results.title: How to Study for Exams — A Research-Backed Strategy | Revaldo AI
results.author: Revaldo AI
results.text:
How to Study for Exams — A Research-Backed Strategy | Revaldo AI

Exam Prep · Spaced Repetition · Active Recall

# How to Study for Exams:A Strategy That Actually Works

Most students spend 80% of their study time on passive review — re-reading and highlighting. Research is clear that this is one of the least effective approaches. This guide gives you a timing-based exam strategy built on the two most evidence-backed techniques: spaced repetition and active recall.

Build my exam study plan See the strategy

Free — no credit card AI-generated study plans Practice tests included

Everything you need to prepare for your exam

AI Study PlansEnter your exam date and get a full day-by-day preparation schedule built automatically.

AI Quiz GeneratorTurn your notes into practice tests. MCQ and short-answer, AI-graded instantly.

AI Flashcard GeneratorAuto-generate spaced repetition flashcards from your lecture notes and readings.

Feynman TechniqueIdentify gaps in understanding before the exam — explain from memory, get AI feedback.

AI Summary + ChatCompress large topics into concise summaries, then quiz yourself on them.

Build your exam planFree. Enter your exam date and start your personalized prep today.

## Why Most Exam Preparation Doesn't Work

Two behaviors kill most students' exam results: starting too late and using passive study methods. Both are predictable and avoidable.

Starting late collapses available time below what spaced repetition requires. You cannot meaningfully space reviews across 3 days when an exam is tomorrow. And passive methods — re-reading notes, highlighting, watching lecture videos again — create familiarity, not retrieval ability. The exam tests retrieval. Your study sessions need to too.

The research on this is unambiguous. In Dunlosky et al.'s (2013) comprehensive review of study techniques, practice testing ranked highest for utility and re-reading ranked near the bottom. Yet surveys consistently find re-reading is the most popular study method among students.

3–4 weeks

Minimum lead time to use spaced repetition effectively for a major exam. Starting later forces cramming — with the retention consequences that follow.

## The 4-Phase Exam Preparation Framework

This framework divides exam preparation into four distinct phases, each with a specific job to do. The system assumes you're starting 3–4 weeks out from the exam.

1

Weeks 3–4 before

### Phase 1 — Active Learning

Read and understand all material. Take structured notes. Create your flashcard sets. Don't try to memorize yet — your job in this phase is to process and organize the content. Upload notes to Revaldo AI to auto-generate flashcards and summaries.

2

Week 2 before

### Phase 2 — First Review Round

Your first spaced repetition pass through all flashcards and key concepts. Do your first practice test per topic without notes. Grade yourself honestly. Flag everything you got wrong — these are high-priority for Phase 3.

3

Days 3–7 before

### Phase 3 — Targeted Retrieval

Second pass through all material, with emphasis on flagged weak areas from Phase 2. Practice test again. By now you should be getting most things right — if not, prioritize those areas above everything else. The goal is to enter exam week confident on 80%+ of the material.

4

Last 48 hours

### Phase 4 — Consolidation and Rest

Light review only — no new information. Work through your weakest flashcards once. Review your own error patterns. Sleep is critical: memory consolidation happens during sleep, and cognitive performance on the day requires it. No all-nighters.

## What to Do When You're Starting Late

If you're starting with less than a week before the exam, you can't use spaced repetition at full effect. But you can still use active recall, which is where the biggest performance gains come from even in a short timeframe.

### The 72-hour emergency plan:

1

### Map the exam

List every topic on the exam. Estimate the mark % per topic. Identify which 20% of topics likely make up 60%+ of marks — start there.

2

### Create flashcards fast

Upload your notes to Revaldo AI. Get flashcard sets in minutes. Don't manually create cards — that time is better spent on retrieval.

3

### Prioritize testing

Spend at least 60% of your study time on active retrieval — flashcards and practice questions. Not reading. Retrieval produces 2× better retention than re-reading in the same time.

4

### Sleep the night before

This is non-negotiable. Sleep consolidates everything you studied. A rested brain outperforms a sleep-deprived one on tests even if the rested brain studied less.

## How RevaldoAI Makes Exam Prep Faster

The biggest time drain in exam preparation is converting study materials into active recall tools — making flashcards, writing practice questions, building a schedule. Revaldo AI automates all of this:

- AI Study Plans: Enter your exam date and subjects — get a complete, day-by-day study schedule with spaced review sessions automatically distributed.
- AI Flashcard Generator: Upload lecture notes, PDFs, or pasted text — get a complete flashcard set in under 60 seconds. 10× faster than making cards manually.
- AI Quiz Generator: Turn any notes into a full practice test with MCQ and short-answer questions. AI grades every answer and explains what you got wrong.

Related Tools

## Your exam prep, automated.

Let Revaldo AI generate your study plan, flashcards and practice tests — so you spend your time on retrieval, not preparation.

### AI Study Plans

Enter your exam date. Get a complete daily study schedule with spaced review sessions built in automatically. Plan my exam prep

### AI Quiz Generator

Generate practice tests from your notes instantly. AI grades every answer with specific feedback on what you got wrong. Create practice tests

### Spaced Repetition Guide

Understand how the spacing effect works and how to use it to cut your study time in half while retaining more. Read the guide

FAQ

## Questions about exam preparation?

How far in advance should I start studying for an exam?

For a major exam, starting 3–4 weeks in advance gives you enough time to use spaced repetition effectively. For a smaller test, 1–2 weeks is usually sufficient. The key constraint is that spaced repetition requires multiple review sessions spaced over time — you can't compress this into one or two days and get the same result.

Is cramming ever a good idea?

Cramming can boost performance on a test tomorrow, but the information is typically forgotten within days. If this is a standalone exam and you will never need the information again, cramming might be pragmatic. But for anything cumulative — courses that build on prior material, professional qualifications, language learning — cramming creates a permanently deteriorating knowledge base.

What should I do the night before an exam?

The night before, do a light review of your notes and flashcards — no new material. Focus only on your weakest areas. Prioritize sleep above all else: sleep is critical for memory consolidation and cognitive performance on the exam. A well-rested brain consistently outperforms one that stayed up all night cramming.

What is the most effective way to study for a multiple-choice exam?

Practice testing under conditions similar to the actual exam. Work through past papers or practice questions without looking at notes. Time yourself. Review only what you got wrong. Research consistently shows that practice testing is the most effective preparation for any exam format, and particularly effective for multiple-choice because it directly practises recognition and elimination.

How do I make a study plan for an exam?

Start from the exam date and work backward. List all topics and estimate how much content needs covering. Allocate 60–70% of your time to initial learning and 30–40% to active recall review sessions spaced across the final weeks. Revaldo AI's study plan generator does this automatically — enter your exam date and it builds the schedule for you.

## Your exam prep,built in minutes.

Enter your exam date and let Revaldo AI build your complete day-by-day preparation schedule — with spaced flashcards, practice tests, and review sessions automatically distributed.

results.title: How to Study for Exams: A Step-by-Step System That Works | Notesmakr
results.author: Notesmakr
results.text:
How to Study for Exams: A Step-by-Step System That Works | Notesmakr

Here's the uncomfortable truth: most students don't have a study problem. They have a system problem. They sit down with their notes, re-read them, highlight things in different colours, and then wonder why the knowledge evaporates the moment the exam begins.

You already know how to study for exams in the loosest sense. You open a book, you read it. But reading isn't studying. And highlighting isn't learning. The research on this is unambiguous: passive review strategies feel productive but produce dramatically worse results than active ones.

This guide gives you a complete, step-by-step exam study system grounded in decades of cognitive science. Whether you have three weeks or three days, this system will help you learn more in less time. And actually remember it on exam day.

---

## Why Most Exam Study Strategies Fail

Before we get to the system, we need to understand why the default approach fails.

Most students study using low-utility strategies — a term from Dunlosky et al. (2013), one of the most comprehensive reviews of learning research ever published. Their team evaluated 10 common study techniques and found that the most popular ones (re-reading, highlighting, summarising) are also among the least effective for long-term retention.

Why? Because these strategies rely on recognition, not recall. Recognition is easy: you see something familiar and your brain registers it. Recall is hard: you have to pull information out of your memory from scratch. And exams almost always test recall.

The good news? The science also identifies exactly which strategies do work. They're not complicated. They're just less comfortable than re-reading, which is precisely why most students avoid them.

🔑KEY CONCEPT

The strategies that feel hardest while studying (struggling to recall answers, getting questions wrong, working through difficult problems) are the ones that produce the greatest learning gains. This is called desirable difficulty.

---

## The 7-Step Exam Study System

This is a complete system. You don't need to do everything perfectly the first time, but the more of it you implement, the better your results.

---

1

Understand the Exam Before You Open a Single Textbook

The biggest mistake students make is diving into study materials without first understanding what they're preparing for. Before you do anything else, answer these five questions:

1. What format is the exam? Multiple choice, essay, problem-solving, open book, practical?
2. What topics are weighted most heavily? Check past papers or ask your lecturer.
3. How long do you have? Calculate total days × available hours = study budget.
4. What do you already know? A brief diagnostic quiz saves hours of studying things you've already mastered.
5. What does "passing" look like? Set a target score, then work backwards.

Once you know this, collect all your materials: lecture notes, readings, handouts, past exam papers. Past papers are gold. They reveal what your examiner thinks is important and how they ask questions.

2

Build a Study Schedule (With Buffer Time)

Random studying without a schedule leads to two predictable outcomes: you spend too long on topics you already know, and you run out of time for the hard stuff.

If you haven't built a weekly routine yet, start with our guide on how to create a study schedule that you'll actually stick to — then come back here to apply it to exam prep.

A good exam study schedule has three components:

- Topic prioritisation: Based on your past paper analysis, rank topics by frequency and your current weakness. Spend 70% of your time on high-priority topics.
- Spaced blocks: Study in 45-90 minute focused sessions, not marathon cramming sessions. Research on spaced repetition shows that spreading study sessions over multiple days dramatically outperforms cramming the same total hours.
- Buffer time: Schedule only 70% of your available time as study. The other 30% is for review, unexpected gaps, and rest.

The 5-Day Study Plan (for a one-week timeline): Day 1 is priority topics. Day 2 is practice testing on Day 1 content plus new topics. Day 3 is reviewing weak spots plus new topics. Day 4 is a full past paper simulation. Day 5 is light review and rest.

💡TIP

Schedule your hardest subjects when your energy is highest. That's typically the morning or after exercise. Your willpower is a finite resource. Don't waste peak cognitive energy on easy review tasks.

3

Engage With Material Using Active Recall

This is the single most impactful change you can make to your study system.

Active recall means retrieving information from memory without looking at your notes. Instead of re-reading a page, you close the book and try to write down everything you remember. Instead of highlighting a definition, you look away and explain it in your own words.

The research behind this is overwhelming. Roediger & Karpicke (2006) found that students who used practice testing retained 50% more information after one week compared to students who restudied the same material. A meta-analysis of 118 studies confirmed that testing improves retention by an average of 50%, with effects lasting months or years.

How to do active recall during a study session:

- Read a section of notes, then close them and write everything you remember
- Turn headings into questions and answer them from memory
- Use AI flashcards to generate quiz questions from your notes in seconds
- Practice explaining concepts out loud to an imaginary student
- For exams that include multiple choice sections, drill a deliberate multiple choice test strategy so distractors do not steal points on test day

The discomfort you feel when you can't remember something? That's the learning happening. Don't look at the answer immediately. Struggle for at least 30 seconds first. The struggle is the mechanism. Adopting a growth mindset helps you view that struggle as progress, not failure.

4

Use Spaced Repetition to Schedule Reviews

Active recall tells you what to study. Spaced repetition tells you when to study it again.

The forgetting curve, discovered by psychologist Hermann Ebbinghaus, shows that we forget roughly 50% of new information within 24 hours, and up to 80% within a week. But every time you review information just before you're about to forget it, the forgetting curve resets and gets shallower. Over time, you need to review material less and less often.

Practically, this means:

- Review new material the same day you learn it
- Review again after 1 day, 3 days, 1 week, 2 weeks
- Flag difficult items for more frequent review; easy items for less frequent review

Notesmakr's AI flashcards handle this automatically. The app generates flashcards from your notes and schedules reviews using spaced repetition algorithms, so you spend the right amount of time on each concept instead of guessing. If you'd like to understand the science more deeply, our guide on the forgetting curve explains exactly how Ebbinghaus's research translates to a modern study system.

5

Test Yourself With Past Papers and Practice Questions

There's a difference between "feeling prepared" and "being prepared." Past papers close that gap.

Practice testing is consistently rated one of the two highest-utility study strategies in Dunlosky et al.'s (2013) landmark review (along with spaced repetition). Why? Because it:

- Reveals exactly where your knowledge gaps are
- Conditions you to the exam format and time pressure
- Activates retrieval practice (same mechanism as active recall)
- Reduces exam anxiety by making the format familiar

How to use past papers effectively:

1. Complete the paper under timed, realistic conditions. No notes, no phone.
2. Mark your answers rigorously. Don't give yourself partial credit.
3. For every wrong answer, identify why you got it wrong. Was it a knowledge gap? A misread question? Time pressure?
4. Go back to your notes and use active recall specifically on the gaps revealed.
5. Better yet, do practice tests with classmates. Studying in a group with real-time quizzes adds competitive pressure that keeps everyone focused and reveals gaps faster than solo review.

✏️TRY THIS

Pull out the most recent past paper for your exam right now. Mark up every question by topic. Which topics appear most? Which do you feel least confident about? This 20-minute exercise will completely reshape your study priorities.

6

Use the Feynman Technique for Concepts You Don't Understand

Not everything can be memorised with flashcards. Some topics (particularly in science, mathematics, economics, and philosophy) require genuine understanding, not just recall.

For these, use the Feynman Technique:

1. Write the concept at the top of a blank page
2. Explain it as if teaching a 12-year-old. No jargon, no shortcuts, plain language only.
3. Find where your explanation breaks down. Those are your gaps.
4. Go back to your source material, fill the gap, then re-explain.

The key insight: if you can't explain something simply, you don't understand it. You've only memorised the words. The Feynman Technique forces you to discover this before the exam does.

This technique is particularly powerful when combined with AI-assisted note-taking. Write your Feynman explanation in Notesmakr, then let the AI generate flashcards and quiz questions based on your own explanation. You're reinforcing understanding and recall simultaneously.

7

Protect Your Recovery: Sleep, Movement, and Single-Tasking

This step is typically ignored. It shouldn't be.

Sleep: Memory consolidation happens during sleep, specifically during slow-wave and REM sleep phases. A study by Walker & Stickgold (2004) found that sleep after learning improves memory retention by up to 40% compared to staying awake. Pulling an all-nighter before an exam is one of the worst decisions you can make. You'll remember less and perform worse on the day. If you must study while sleep-deprived, our guide on how to study when tired covers the strategies that actually preserve retention despite the fatigue.

Exercise: Even a 20-minute walk increases blood flow to the hippocampus, the brain region responsible for memory formation. Schedule movement breaks between study sessions, not as a reward for finishing, but as a tool to help you study better.

Single-tasking: A Stanford study found that chronic multitaskers perform significantly worse on memory tasks compared to people who focus on one thing at a time. Phone off. One tab open. One task. The cost of switching between tasks is far higher than most students realise.

⚠️WARNING

Pulling an all-nighter before a major exam may feel like dedication, but it actively impairs the memory consolidation you spent weeks building. Aim for 7-9 hours of sleep the night before. Nothing you could review in those hours is worth the cognitive penalty.

---

## Watch: The Science of Studying Smarter

Sometimes hearing the research explained out loud cements it better than reading. Here are two outstanding video explanations of evidence-based study techniques:

### Study Less Study Smart — Marty Lobdell

Marty Lobdell — Study Less Study Smart (Pierce College)

Psychology professor Marty Lobdell has spent 40 years teaching students how to study effectively. This iconic lecture covers focus, memory, and active learning. Key insight: most students study for too long in a single session. Shorter, more focused sessions with review produce dramatically better results.

### How to Study for Exams — Spaced Repetition — Ali Abdaal

Ali Abdaal on evidence-based exam study using spaced repetition

Ali Abdaal, a former Cambridge medical student turned productivity educator, walks through the spaced repetition system he used to score in the top percentiles of his medical exams. Key insight: reviewing information at the right time intervals is more important than how many total hours you study.

---

## How AI Flashcards Supercharge Every Step of This System

You can implement every step in this guide manually. But if you want to move significantly faster, AI flashcards compress the time it takes to build study materials and schedule reviews.

Here's how Notesmakr's AI tools support each phase of the system:

| Study Phase | Manual Approach | With Notesmakr AI |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Creating study materials | Write flashcards by hand (60-90 min/hour of notes) | AI generates flashcards from your |
| notes in under a minute |
| Active recall | Self-quiz from memory with paper cards | AI quizzes you interactively with instant feedback |
| Spaced repetition | Maintain a manual review schedule with boxes/dates | Spaced repetition scheduling happens |
| automatically |
| Identifying gaps | Notice gaps during self-quiz, manually re-study | AI highlights weak areas and re-routes review |
| Feynman explanations | Write in a notebook, self-assess | AI generates questions to test your explanation |

To get started, upload any set of notes (lecture slides, PDFs, handwritten photos) to Notesmakr's AI flashcard maker. The AI does the heavy lifting so you can spend your study time actually studying, not building study materials.

✏️TRY THIS

After your next lecture or reading session, take a photo of your notes or paste them into Notesmakr. Within 60 seconds you'll have a complete set of AI-generated flashcards. Study them that evening, then again 3 days later. Notice how much more you remember.

---

## Common Exam Study Mistakes (And How to Fix Them)

### Mistake 1: Studying Everything Equally

Most exams are not evenly weighted. Some topics appear far more frequently than others. Students who spend equal time on all material often know low-priority topics deeply and high-priority topics superficially.

The fix: Do a past paper topic frequency analysis first. Allocate study time proportionally to exam weight, not alphabetically or by chapter order.

### Mistake 2: Confusing Familiarity With Knowledge

After re-reading your notes three times, everything feels familiar. You think you know it. But familiarity is not recall. On exam day, when you're asked to produce the answer rather than recognise it, the familiar feeling vanishes.

The fix: Always test yourself with active recall before deciding you "know" something. If you can reproduce the concept accurately without looking at your notes, you know it. If not, study more.

❌ PASSIVE REVIEW — What Most Students Do

Re-reads notes three times. "I feel like I understand photosynthesis now. I've read it and it all makes sense."

On exam day: "I know I've seen this before but I can't remember the steps..."

✅ ACTIVE RECALL — What Works

Closes notes and tries to write the full process of photosynthesis from scratch. Gets stuck on the light-dependent reaction. Opens notes to fill the gap. Tests again the next day.

On exam day: "I've reconstructed this from memory six times. I've got this."

### Mistake 3: Starting Too Late

Spaced repetition requires time to work. If you start studying three days before a major exam, you have time for at most two review cycles per concept. Start three weeks out and you have time for five or six. That's dramatically stronger retention.

The fix: As soon as you know your exam date, schedule your first study session. Even if it's just 30 minutes of reading. Starting early beats studying more intensively every single time. If you keep putting off that first session, read our guide on how to beat procrastination before exams for science-backed strategies to break the delay cycle.

### Mistake 4: Studying in Long, Unbroken Sessions

Three hours of continuous studying sounds impressive. Cognitively, it's counterproductive. Attention and working memory capacity decline sharply after 45-90 minutes without a break, meaning the last hour is often near-worthless.

The fix: Use the Pomodoro Technique. Forty-five minute focused blocks with 10-minute breaks. Four blocks equals a highly productive half-day of studying that beats eight hours of unfocused grind.

### Mistake 5: Studying Without Output

Studying in silence, just reading and thinking, produces weak memory traces. Studying with output (writing, speaking, drawing, explaining) produces strong ones. The act of output forces your brain to retrieve and reconstruct information rather than passively receive it.

The fix: Always do something with the material. Write summaries from memory. Explain concepts aloud. Draw diagrams. Generate questions. Create AI flashcards from your notes. Build a study guide that organizes everything into one reviewable document. Output is studying. Input is just reading.

---

## Quick Reference: Which Technique for Which Situation

| Situation | Best Technique |
| --- | --- |
| 3+ weeks before the exam | Start spaced repetition with active recall immediately |
| Learning a complex concept | Feynman Technique: explain it simply until gaps disappear |
| Organising lecture notes for review | Cornell note-taking method: cue column + |
| weekly review |
| 1-2 weeks out | Past paper simulations plus targeted active recall on gaps |
| 2-3 days out | Light spaced repetition review; no new material |
| Night before | Sleep 7-9 hours. Seriously. No cramming. |
| During the exam | Skip questions you're stuck on; return later; answer everything |

---

## The Research Behind the System

This system isn't a productivity hack. It's grounded in decades of rigorous learning science:

- Practice Testing / Retrieval Practice (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006): Students who used practice testing retained 50% more information after one week compared to students who restudied.
- Distributed Practice / Spaced Repetition (Cepeda et al., 2006): Spacing study sessions over time produces significantly better long-term retention than massed practice.
- High-Utility vs Low-Utility Strategies (Dunlosky et al., 2013): Practice testing and distributed practice are the only two "high utility" strategies out of 10 reviewed. Re-reading and highlighting rated "low utility."
- Sleep and Memory Consolidation (Walker & Stickgold, 2004): Sleep after learning improves memory retention by up to 40%. Sleep deprivation before an exam causes measurable performance decline.
- The Spacing Effect (Ebbinghaus, 1885): Ebbinghaus's original forgetting curve research showed that spaced review sessions combat forgetting more effectively than equivalent time spent in a single session.

---

## How Notesmakr Helps You Apply This System

Notesmakr is an AI-powered study app designed around exactly the techniques in this guide. Here's what it provides:

- AI flashcard generation: Upload any notes, PDF, or text and get a complete flashcard deck in under 60 seconds. No more hours spent manually writing cards.
- AI quiz generation: The app creates quiz questions from your notes to drive active recall practice automatically.
- Spaced repetition scheduling: The system schedules when you review each card based on your performance. Harder cards come back sooner, mastered cards less often.
- Feynman Technique integration: Write your Feynman explanations in Notesmakr and get AI-generated questions that test whether your understanding is complete.
- Mind mapping: For subjects where understanding connections matters, the AI generates visual mind maps from your notes to reveal how concepts relate.

Try Notesmakr free at notesmakr.com. No credit card required. Upload today's lecture notes and have your first flashcard set ready to study in under two minutes.

---

## Frequently Asked Questions About Studying for Exams

### What is the most effective way to study for an exam?

The most effective way to study for an exam combines active recall and spaced repetition. Active recall means testing yourself on material rather than re-reading it. This forces retrieval, which strengthens memory. Spaced repetition means spacing review sessions over days and weeks rather than cramming. Research by Dunlosky et al. (2013) rated these as the only two "high utility" strategies out of ten studied.

### How many days before an exam should I start studying?

Start at least two to three weeks before a major exam. Spaced repetition requires multiple review sessions spread over time. Starting two weeks out gives you time for five to six review cycles per concept, versus one or two if you start three days out. For cumulative exams or finals, some students begin reviewing material from week one of the semester. If finals week is approaching fast, our last-minute finals study guide covers triage strategies for when time is short. If you have several exams in the same week, our guide on how to study for multiple exams at once covers scheduling and prioritisation strategies specifically for that situation.

### Does cramming actually work for exams?

Cramming produces short-term recall but extremely poor long-term retention. You may remember enough to pass a test the next morning, but the information typically disappears within days. Cramming also fails for complex conceptual material where understanding (not just memorisation) is tested. It works best only for very simple factual lists in low-stakes, short-term contexts.

### How do I study if I don't understand the material?

Use the Feynman Technique: explain the concept in plain language as if teaching someone with no prior knowledge. When your explanation breaks down (when you reach for jargon or get vague), that's your gap. Return to your source material, fill the gap, then explain again. Repeat until you can explain the concept clearly without notes. This forces genuine understanding rather than surface memorisation.

### How do I stay focused while studying?

Remove distractions before starting: phone on Do Not Disturb, unnecessary tabs closed, notifications off. Study in focused blocks of 45-90 minutes using the Pomodoro Technique, followed by a genuine break (walk, stretch, water, not more screen time). Your environment matters too. A consistent, clutter-free study space conditions your brain to enter focused mode faster. For the complete science-backed system for maintaining concentration throughout your exam prep, read How to Focus While Studying.

---

## Start Today: Your 10-Minute Action Plan

You don't need to implement the entire system at once. Here's what to do in the next 10 minutes:

1. Write down your next exam date and calculate how many days you have
2. Find the most recent past paper for that exam. If it doesn't exist, ask your lecturer for sample questions.
3. Do a topic frequency analysis: mark up each question by topic (takes about 15 minutes)
4. Open Notesmakr and upload your most recent set of notes. Let the AI generate your first flashcard set.
5. Schedule your next three study sessions in your calendar right now, with a time, a topic, and a method (active recall, past paper, or Feynman technique)
6. Set a sleep alarm for every night until your exam. Eight hours before your desired wake time.

"The secret of getting ahead is getting started."

— Mark Twain

The system in this guide has helped students at every level, from secondary school to medical school. If you're preparing for AP exams specifically, our subject-by-subject AP exam study guide applies these same techniques to every AP course. The difference between students who consistently outperform their peers isn't intelligence. It's that they have a system. Now you have one too.

Looking for a complete system to put all of this into practice? Our how to study for exams guide shows you exactly how to combine AI flashcards, active recall, and spaced repetition into a step-by-step exam preparation system.

---

## The Research: Full Citations

- Dunlosky, J., Rawson, K.A., Marsh, E.J., Nathan, M.J., & Willingham, D.T. (2013). "Improving Students' Learning With Effective Learning Techniques: Promising Directions From Cognitive and Educational Psychology." Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 14(1), 4–58. PubMed
- Roediger, H.L., & Karpicke, J.D. (2006). "Test-enhanced learning: Taking memory tests improves long-term retention." Psychological Science, 17(3), 249–255.
- Cepeda, N.J., Pashler, H., Vul, E., Wixted, J.T., & Rohrer, D. (2006). "Distributed practice in verbal recall tasks: A review and quantitative synthesis." Psychological Bulletin, 132(3), 354–380.
- Walker, M.P., & Stickgold, R. (2004). "Sleep-dependent learning and memory consolidation." Neuron, 44(1), 121–133.
- Ebbinghaus, H. (1885). Über das Gedächtnis (Memory: A Contribution to Experimental Psychology). Leipzig: Duncker & Humblot.

The fundamental insight: Studying more is not the answer. Studying better is. Replace passive review with active retrieval. Replace marathon sessions with spaced practice. Replace familiarity with tested recall. The techniques in this guide have been validated across hundreds of studies. They work regardless of subject, age, or starting ability.

results.title: How to Create a Study Plan for an Exam: Step-by-Step + Templates (2026) | okti Blog
results.author: okti Team
results.text:
How to Create a Study Plan for an Exam: Step-by-Step + Templates (2026) | okti Blog

# How to Create a Study Plan for an Exam: Step-by-Step + Templates (2026)

A study plan is not a pretty spreadsheet. A study plan is a stress-reduction system that turns “I should study” into daily actions you can actually execute.

Without a plan, most students drift into two traps:

1. They spend too much time on what feels easy (re-reading, highlighting, summarizing), and
2. they realize too late what the exam really tests.

This guide shows you a practical method to build a plan in 30–60 minutes — one that is realistic, prioritized, and built around retrieval + repetition.

You’ll get:

- a step-by-step planning framework,
- 7/14/28-day templates,
- a simple prioritization rule (80/20),
- common mistakes (and fixes),
- and a workflow to turn notes/PDFs into questions + spaced repetition.

## Table of contents

- What a good study plan must do (and what it must avoid)
- Step 1: Lock the constraints (date, format, scope)
- Step 2: Break the syllabus into usable blocks
- Step 3: Prioritize for points (80/20)
- Step 4: Budget time realistically (with buffer)
- Step 5: Convert “study” into concrete outputs (Active Recall)
- Step 6: Schedule repetition (Spaced Repetition)
- Templates: 7-day, 14-day, and 28-day study plans
- Bonus: Study plan checklist
- Planning for multiple exams
- Quick comparison: paper vs spreadsheets vs Notion vs an app
- Common mistakes (and how to fix them)
- Tools: how okti fits into your study plan
- FAQ
- Conclusion

---

## What a good study plan must do (and what it must avoid)

A good plan has three jobs:

1. Exam alignment: it trains what the exam demands (problem types, recall, explanation).
2. Realism: it fits your actual week, includes buffer, and can survive a bad day.
3. Adaptability: it can be updated weekly without rebuilding everything.

What a good plan avoids:

- planning only input (reading, watching videos),
- zero buffer time,
- too much detail (micro-tasks for every 15 minutes),
- and “one-and-done” studying without repetition.

Key sentence

Your plan is only as good as your repetition. If repetition isn’t scheduled, it won’t happen.

---

## Step 1: Lock the constraints (date, format, scope)

Before you write anything into a calendar, make these five things explicit:

1. Exam date + time (and whether there are multiple parts)
2. Format: multiple choice, essays, calculations, oral exam, open book?
3. Weighting: which topics matter most? Do you have past papers?
4. Materials: slides, script, problem sets, tutorials, notes
5. Time window: how many hours/week is realistic?

Different formats require different training:

- math-heavy exams → lots of practice problems
- oral exams → explanation, structure, speaking drills
- factual exams → recall + spaced repetition

If you have past papers, they’re gold. If not, your best proxies are problem sets and tutorial sheets.

---

## Step 2: Break the syllabus into usable blocks

Most study plans die here because the blocks are either too big (“chapters 1–10”) or too tiny (300 micro-topics).

Target 10–25 blocks. Each block should be doable in 1–3 sessions and have a clear output.

How to do it:

- start from the syllabus / lecture list,
- group by concept (not by page number),
- note prerequisites (what depends on what).

### Example: blocks that are actually schedulable

---

## Step 3: Prioritize for points (80/20)

If you treat every topic equally, you’ll either:

- run out of time, or
- repeat too little.

Prioritization means investing where your score improves most.

A simple scoring method:

Give each block three scores (1–5):

- Exam relevance (how often it shows up)
- Difficulty (for you)
- Confidence (can you explain it without notes?)

High priority = high relevance + low confidence / high difficulty.

Common trap

Students plan based on “what’s next in the script” instead of “what wins points”. Your plan is a points strategy, not a reading schedule.

If you have past papers:

- list recurring question types,
- collect the “top 15” tasks you must be able to do,
- build the plan around training these tasks repeatedly.

---

## Extra: Build your plan from past papers (fastest route to points)

If you have access to past papers (or even just one sample exam), treat it as the “map” your study plan should follow. A surprisingly effective workflow is:

1. Collect 3–10 past papers (or as many as you can)
2. Tag each question by topic block (the blocks you created in Step 2)
3. Create a frequency list: which blocks appear the most?
4. For each high-frequency block, write down the typical tasks (e.g., “derive formula X”, “solve type-Y problems”, “explain definition Z with an example”)

Your plan then becomes very concrete:

- build skill on the common tasks,
- drill them repeatedly,
- and use your error list to steer repetition.

Past paper rule

A topic you see repeatedly in past papers deserves repetition. A topic you never see deserves a smaller time budget.

If you don’t have past papers, you can approximate this with:

- tutorial sheets,
- end-of-chapter problem sets,
- and what your instructor emphasized in class.

## Step 4: Budget time realistically (with buffer)

### 1) Plan for net time, not fantasy time

A “3-hour study block” rarely yields 3 net hours. Food, breaks, low focus, and life happen.

A good default: schedule only 70% of your available time as study time. The other 30% is buffer.

### 2) Add repetition slots from day one

Repetition isn’t optional. Put it on the calendar:

- quick review: next day (10–20 min)
- second review: 3–4 days later
- third review: 7 days later
- final review: 2–3 days before the exam

### 3) Use a stop rule

Without a stop rule, you overperfect one topic.

Examples:

- “After 90 minutes I move on.”
- “When I can solve 70% of typical problems, I switch.”

---

## Step 5: Convert “study” into concrete outputs (Active Recall)

If your tasks are vague, you’ll procrastinate.

Avoid these:

- ❌ “read chapter 3”
- ❌ “review slides”
- ❌ “study lecture 5”

Use measurable outputs instead:

- ✅ “answer 20 questions (no notes)”
- ✅ “solve 10 typical problems”
- ✅ “explain the concept out loud in 2 minutes”
- ✅ “write a 15-minute mini-test”

Rule of thumb

If the task has no output you can check, it’s not a study task — it’s a hope.

A simple block structure that works for most subjects:

1. Input (short): 20–40 min
2. Output (long): 40–90 min
3. Review (short): 10–20 min the next day

---

## Step 6: Schedule repetition (Spaced Repetition)

Spaced repetition is the easiest “force multiplier” for exams. It turns repetition into a predictable daily routine.

Minimal version (works even when you’re busy):

- daily: 15–25 min flashcards/questions
- weekly: one longer review (60–120 min)

### Turn your materials into repeatable questions

With okti you can quickly create flashcards and quiz questions from PDFs and slides — ideal for spaced repetition inside your study plan.

Try okti for free

---

## Make the plan stick: anti-procrastination tactics

A plan that looks good but never gets executed is just guilt in calendar form. Here are tactics that reliably increase follow-through:

Lower the start friction (2-minute start): define the smallest possible first action.

- Example: “Open the problem set and solve one question.”
- Once you start, momentum usually follows.

Use “next action” tasks, not projects:

- Bad: “Study statistics.”
- Good: “Answer 15 questions on hypothesis tests (no notes).”

Protect one daily anchor: pick a time that’s predictable (morning or after lunch) and reserve 20 minutes for repetition. If your day falls apart, you still keep the habit.

Plan for bad days: schedule buffer and define what “minimum viable studying” looks like.

- Example minimum: 10 minutes review + 1 small output task.

Reward completion, not perfection: stop rules (90 minutes) prevent endless polishing and keep you moving.

Planning trap

If you keep “optimizing the template”, you’re often avoiding the discomfort of retrieval practice. The plan is supposed to make you do the hard thing.

## Templates: 7-day, 14-day, and 28-day study plans

Templates are only helpful when they include priorities and repetition.

### 7-day template (last minute, but smart)

If you have only 7 days, think in question types, not chapters.

Day 2–5: high priority loop

- morning: 20 min review
- main block: output-heavy practice
- evening: update the error list

Last 48 hours

Don’t stack new knowledge right before the exam. Train retrieval and fix gaps.

### 14-day template (the classic)

A helpful daily structure:

- Day 7: light review + sleep
- 10 min: set the output goal
- 30 min: input
- 60–90 min: output
- 10–20 min: update your error list

Week 1: build + first repetition

- day 1: planning + block 1
- day 2–6: 1–2 blocks/day
- day 7: weekly review + mini-test

Week 2: exam training + consolidation

- day 8–11: practice sets / past papers
- day 12: weak spots day
- day 13: full simulation + analysis
- day 14: light review + recovery

### 28-day template (calm + high performance)

This is where spaced repetition really shines:

- week 1: build foundations + first questions
- week 2: output-intensive practice + card creation
- week 3: repetition + simulations
- week 4: weak spots + final review

Plan at least one full rest day per week.

---

## Bonus: Study plan checklist

Use this as a final quality check:

- exam date/format is clear
- 10–25 topic blocks exist
- high priority blocks are identified (≥ 5)
- every block has an output task
- daily repetition is scheduled (15–25 min)
- 30% buffer is kept free
- at least 1 rest day/week
- simulations are scheduled
- an error list (“top gaps”) is maintained

Quick win

If you’re overwhelmed: schedule just two things today — 20 minutes of review and one 90-minute output block. Scale later.

---

## Planning for multiple exams

When you have 2–5 exams at once, the goal is not equal time per subject. The goal is stability + points.

A simple approach:

1. Per subject: limit yourself to 3–6 blocks (not 30)
2. Per day: maximum 2 subjects (context switching kills focus)
3. Keep one daily repetition routine (flashcards/questions) across subjects

Example week (3 exams):

- Mon: Subject A (output) + review
- Tue: Subject B (output) + review
- Wed: Subject A (problem sets) + review
- Thu: Subject C (output) + review
- Fri: Subject B (problem sets) + review
- Sat: simulation (rotate A/B/C)
- Sun: rest / light review

---

## Quick comparison: paper vs spreadsheets vs Notion vs an app

| Tool | Fast to start? | Easy to adjust? | Repetition built-in? | Daily friction |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Paper / calendar | Very | Medium | No | Low |
| Spreadsheet | Medium | High | No | Medium |
| Notion template | Medium | Very | No | High |
| oktiEmpfohlen | High | High | Yes | Low |

Interpretation:

- paper is great for simple structure,
- spreadsheets are great if you like planning,
- Notion is great if you don’t fall into “setup mode”,
- an app is great when it makes repetition automatic.

---

## Common mistakes (and how to fix them)

### Mistake 1: Planning only input

Fix: every session needs a measurable output.

### Mistake 2: No buffer

Fix: keep 30% free. Your future self will thank you.

### Mistake 3: Over-detailing

Fix: 1–3 main blocks per day + repetition. That’s enough.

### Mistake 4: Ignoring energy levels

Fix: schedule the hardest blocks on your best energy days.

### Mistake 5: No error list

Fix: maintain a “top gaps” list and schedule exactly those.

---

## Tools: how okti fits into your study plan

A plan is easy to write. The time sink is turning raw material into trainable units.

That’s where okti helps:

1. From PDFs/slides to questions fast: instead of rewriting summaries, you generate questions and practice retrieval.
2. Daily repetition without thinking: spaced repetition becomes a default routine.
3. Visibility into gaps: the system shows you what you keep missing.

A simple workflow:

- upload your notes/slides
- create flashcards + quiz questions
- schedule daily review (20 min)
- add 2–3 weekly practice sessions with problem sets/past papers

### Start small, stay consistent

20 minutes per day beats random 6-hour panic sessions. okti helps you make your materials repeatable.

---

## FAQ

### How many hours should I study per day?

For many students, 2–4 net hours works well. Consistency + repetition matters more than marathon sessions.

### Should I reread everything at the end?

Only as a high-level overview. Your score comes from retrieval: problems, questions, flashcards.

### What if I don’t follow my plan?

Then your plan doesn’t match reality — it’s not a character flaw. Do a 10-minute weekly review: what worked, what was too much, what was unnecessary.

### Is an Excel template worth it?

Yes, if it shows three things clearly: 1) topic block, 2) output task, 3) repetition dates. If repetition isn’t visible, it gets forgotten.

### How big should a “topic block” be?

Big enough to matter, small enough to finish. A good target is a block you can complete in 60–180 minutes including at least one output task. If it’s bigger, you’ll keep postponing it. If it’s smaller, your plan becomes noisy and hard to maintain.

### How often should I update the plan?

Weekly is enough. Set a 10-minute “plan review” every Sunday:

- what worked,
- what took longer than expected,
- and which gaps you need to schedule next week.

---

## Conclusion

Creating a study plan is simple. Creating a good study plan means:

- breaking content into usable blocks,
- prioritizing for points,
- planning realistically with buffer,
- and making output + repetition the core.

If you want the minimal version:

1. 15 topic blocks,
2. 5 high priority blocks,
3. one block per day + 20 minutes review,
4. one simulation per week.

Update your plan every Sunday for 10 minutes and it will stay realistic.

And if you want to turn PDFs/slides into repeatable questions faster: okti is built for exactly that — even under pressure. For last-minute prep, see our one-week exam plan and avoiding cramming tips.

results.author: Sidetracked Day
results.text:
How to Study for Exams: The Complete Student Preparation Guide | Sidetracked Day

## Why cramming fails and what to do instead

Question: Why does last-minute cramming produce poor exam results despite feeling productive?

Answer: Cramming floods working memory with information in a short period. You can hold enough of it to pass the exam the next morning, but without repeated retrieval at spaced intervals, that information decays rapidly. Within a week, most of what was crammed is inaccessible. The deeper problem is that cramming gives students false confidence: the material feels familiar immediately after reviewing it, which is not the same as being able to retrieve it reliably under exam pressure three days later.

Research on the spacing effect, first described by Hermann Ebbinghaus and replicated extensively since, consistently shows that the same total amount of study time produces far stronger retention when distributed across multiple sessions than when concentrated in one. Two one-hour sessions spaced three days apart will typically outperform a single two-hour session immediately before an exam, even though the total time is identical.

### Why passive review does not work

Rereading creates familiarity, not recall

When you reread your notes, the material feels easier to process each time. This processing fluency feels like learning but is a distinct cognitive experience from being able to retrieve the information without any cues. Exams test recall, not recognition. As explored in the article on why rereading notes is ineffective, this fluency gap is one of the most consistent findings in educational psychology.

Coverage does not equal mastery

Spending time on every topic equally feels thorough but wastes time on content you already know while leaving gaps where your understanding is genuinely weak. Effective exam preparation concentrates effort on the material that is most likely to appear and where your current recall is lowest.

Studying without feedback hides real gaps

Reading and highlighting give you no information about whether you can actually retrieve the material. Testing yourself does. Every time you cannot answer a practice question from memory, you have identified a gap that passive review would have left completely invisible until the exam.

## How to build an exam study plan that works

Question: How should I structure my study plan in the weeks before an exam?

Answer: An effective exam study plan has three phases: an initial pass through all the material, a targeted review phase focused on weak areas, and a consolidation phase in the final days. Building a concrete schedule using a structured study schedule prevents the common pattern of spending the first week on topics you already know and running out of time for the ones you do not.

When exams are close enough that the calendar matters more than the general weekly routine, switch to a revision timetable built backwards from exam dates. That keeps weak, high-value topics from being crowded out by easier review.

### Phase 1: Initial coverage (weeks two to three before exam)

Work through every topic on the syllabus and past exam papers at least once. Do not try to achieve mastery at this stage. The goal is to identify which topics you understand, which need more work, and which are completely unfamiliar. For each topic, do a short self-test after reviewing it: close your notes and write down the key points from memory. Note your confidence level on each topic. This gives you the data you need to prioritise the next phase.

### Phase 2: Targeted retrieval practice (one to two weeks before exam)

Allocate the majority of your remaining study time to the topics you rated as weakest in Phase 1. Use active recall techniques exclusively: practice questions, past paper answers written without notes, flashcard testing, and the Feynman technique for concepts you find difficult to explain clearly. Review your stronger topics briefly every two to three days to maintain them rather than letting them decay while you focus elsewhere.

### Phase 3: Consolidation (three to four days before exam)

Shift from learning new material to consolidating what you have already reviewed. Complete at least one full past paper under timed conditions. Review your error patterns from practice attempts and spend short focused sessions on the specific points those errors reveal. Avoid trying to learn entirely new topics at this stage. If something important is still completely unfamiliar three days before the exam, make a concise summary of it and focus on understanding the core concept rather than all the detail.

## The most effective exam study techniques

Question: Which study techniques produce the strongest results when preparing for exams?

Answer: Research consistently identifies a small number of techniques as far more effective than the passive methods most students use. The following are ranked by the strength of their supporting evidence.

| Technique | How to use it | Evidence strength |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Active recall | Test yourself without looking at notes; write answers from memory | Very high |
| Spaced repetition | Review each topic at increasing intervals over multiple days | Very high |
| Practice testing | Complete past papers under realistic timed conditions | High |
| Interleaving | Mix topics within a session rather than blocking by subject | High |
| Elaborative interrogation | Ask and answer "why" and "how" for each concept | Moderate to high |
| Rereading and highlighting | Re-read notes or highlight text passages | Low |

The contrast between the top and bottom rows of this table is striking but well established in the research literature. The techniques at the bottom feel easier and more comfortable, which partly explains why students default to them. Effective exam preparation often feels harder in the moment because the difficulty is where the learning happens.

## How to use past papers effectively

Question: What is the best way to use past exam papers when studying?

Answer: Past papers are among the highest-value exam preparation resources available, but most students use them poorly. The most common mistake is to read past papers rather than sit them. Reading a question and then reading the answer tells you very little about whether you could have produced that answer independently. Attempting the question first, under realistic conditions, is what produces learning.

### Step 1: Use them to identify what is tested

Before you start studying, look through several years of past papers and note which topics appear most frequently and which question types recur. This tells you where to concentrate your effort. A topic that appears in every past paper is almost certainly going to appear in yours. A topic that has never appeared in five years of past papers is low priority.

### Step 2: Attempt them under timed conditions

Set a timer, put away your notes, and work through the paper as you would in the real exam. Timed practice does two things that untimed practice cannot: it identifies time management problems before they cost you marks in the real exam, and it replicates the retrieval conditions of the exam itself, which strengthens the memory traces in ways that studying under non-exam conditions does not.

### Step 3: Analyse your errors systematically

After completing a past paper, compare your answers against the mark scheme and classify each error: was it a knowledge gap (you did not know the content), a recall failure (you knew it but could not retrieve it under pressure), or a technique problem (you understood the material but misread the question or ran out of time)? Each category requires a different response, and conflating them leads to studying the wrong thing.

For a reusable template, follow the practice test error log method so repeated mistakes become visible before the real exam.

### Step 4: Review weak areas immediately

After classifying your errors, return to your notes or textbook for only the topics that revealed genuine knowledge gaps. Re-study those sections using active recall rather than passive reading. Then test yourself on those specific topics again within 24 to 48 hours to consolidate the correction.

## Managing focus and avoiding burnout during exam preparation

Question: How do I maintain focus and avoid burning out during exam preparation?

Answer: The most common cause of burnout during exam preparation is not studying too much but studying inefficiently. Passive studying requires long sessions to feel productive, while active recall produces more learning in less time. Switching to active methods naturally reduces the total time you need to study, which reduces the pressure that leads to burnout.

### Use structured study sessions

The Pomodoro technique is particularly effective during exam preparation because it prevents the trap of sitting at your desk for four hours while actually studying productively for one. Work in 25 to 50-minute focused blocks, take a genuine break between each block, and stop studying for the day after a predetermined number of blocks. This structure protects your recovery time and keeps each session genuinely productive.

### Protect sleep above all else

Memory consolidation happens predominantly during sleep. Cutting sleep to create more study hours is one of the most counterproductive things you can do before an exam. Students who sleep for seven to nine hours during exam preparation consistently outperform those who sacrifice sleep for additional revision time, even when the sleep-deprived students studied more total hours. Finish studying at a reasonable hour and treat sleep as part of your exam preparation, not time taken away from it.

### Plan what you will study before you sit down

One of the biggest focus killers during exam preparation is sitting down without a clear plan and then spending the first 20 minutes deciding what to study. Write your plan the evening before each study day: specify the topic, the technique you will use (practice questions, flashcard testing, past paper), and the session length. Starting a session with a concrete task eliminates decision fatigue and gets you into focused work faster.

### Track progress rather than hours

Measuring exam preparation by hours studied encourages passive, low-quality study time to hit a daily target. Measuring by topics covered, practice questions attempted, or past papers completed creates incentives for efficient, active study. At the end of each day, record what you actually achieved rather than how long you sat at your desk.

## What to do in the 24 hours before an exam

Question: How should I use the day before an exam?

Answer: The day before an exam is not the time to learn new material. Use it to consolidate what you already know, protect your sleep, and reduce anxiety. Students who cram until midnight before an exam typically perform worse than those who do a shorter, focused review session earlier in the day and then stop.

### The day-before checklist

One focused review session in the morning

Do a final active recall pass through your highest-priority topics. Use flashcards, summary notes, or self-testing rather than rereading. Keep this session to 90 minutes or less.

Stop studying by early afternoon

Continuing to study into the evening raises anxiety and erodes sleep quality. If you have prepared properly over the previous weeks, everything you need is already in memory. Additional cramming in the final hours adds very little and costs recovery time.

Prepare logistics the evening before

Set out your student ID, pens, calculator, and any permitted materials. Know the exam location and how long the journey takes. Reducing morning decision-making lowers pre-exam anxiety and lets you arrive calm.

Prioritise sleep

Aim for seven to nine hours. Avoid screens for at least an hour before bed. The consolidation that happens during this final night of sleep before the exam is a genuine part of your preparation, not time wasted.

## FAQ: how to study for exams

### What is the most effective way to study for an exam?

Combine active recall and spaced repetition. Start at least two weeks before the exam, test yourself on the material at increasing intervals, and prioritise your weakest topics. Complete at least one past paper under timed conditions and analyse your errors systematically. Avoid spending the majority of your time rereading notes or highlighting.

### How far in advance should I start studying for an exam?

One to two weeks is the minimum for most university exams, giving you time for at least three spaced review cycles per topic. For content-heavy or technical subjects, start three to four weeks out. Finish your first pass through all topics at least one week before the exam so you have time to identify and address gaps.

### Is it better to study a little every day or cram before an exam?

Studying a little every day produces far stronger long-term retention than cramming. The spacing effect shows that distributed practice consolidates memory more effectively than massed practice, even when the total study time is identical. Cramming produces short-term recall that fades quickly. Distributed review builds durable memory.

### How do I know what to focus on when studying for an exam?

Start with past exam papers and your course learning outcomes. These two sources almost always reveal what the examiner considers most important. Then identify the topics where your practice test performance is weakest, and direct the majority of your remaining study time toward those gaps rather than reviewing what you already know.

### How many hours should I study per day during exam preparation?

Three to five focused hours per day is optimal for most students. Beyond five to six hours, study quality drops significantly and the risk of burnout rises. Break your sessions into 25 to 50-minute focused blocks with genuine breaks between them. Consistency across days matters more than total hours in any single session.

## How Sidetracked Day helps you prepare smarter for exams

The biggest challenge in exam preparation is not knowing what to study but knowing what to study next. Students who plan their own revision often over-review the topics they are already comfortable with and underestimate how much time their weak areas need. By the time they discover a serious gap, there is not enough time left to close it.

Sidetracked Day removes this guesswork by tracking your recall performance across every topic and scheduling your review sessions automatically. Specifically, it:

- Identifies which topics you are weakest on based on your actual retrieval performance and automatically schedules those topics for more frequent review before your exam date
- Spaces your review sessions at the intervals that produce the strongest retention for each topic, so you never over-review material you already know or under-review material that is at risk of being forgotten
- Generates active recall questions from your study content so every review session includes retrieval practice rather than passive rereading of notes
- Gives you a clear dashboard of your knowledge state across all your subjects so you can see exactly which topics are exam-ready and which still need attention, without having to guess

The result is exam preparation that uses your available time efficiently, concentrates your effort where it produces the most improvement, and removes the anxiety that comes from not knowing whether you have studied the right things.

## Build a study system that adapts as you learn

Sidetracked Day helps students move beyond static study plans by adapting sessions, practice, and reviews based on how they actually perform.

results.title: How to Study for an Exam: The Science-Backed System That Works (2026)
results.author: Charlie Groves
results.text:
How to Study for an Exam: The Science-Backed System That Works (2026)

By Charlie Groves· Published 9 March 2026

# How to Study for an Exam: The Science-Backed System

What science-backed study techniques for success actually work—essential for anyone following a guide to passing university resits—and why most students study in exactly the wrong way

Last updated March 2026

## Study Techniques Ranked by Research Effectiveness

From Dunlosky et al. (2013) - the most comprehensive meta-analysis of study techniques

HIGH

Practice testing (flashcards, practice questions)

Highly effective across subjects, ages, and retention intervals

HIGH

Distributed practice (spaced repetition)

Spreading study over time dramatically outperforms massed sessions

MOD

Elaborative interrogation (asking "why?")

Generating explanations creates deeper encoding

MOD

Self-explanation (working through why something is true)

Better than passive reading, especially for procedural content

LOW

Re-reading

Creates familiarity, not recall—often leading to mistaking familiarity for true mastery. Very widely used, very low effectiveness.

LOW

Highlighting / underlining

Negligible benefit over passive reading. Widely used, poorly effective.

LOW

Summarisation

Useful for high-prior-knowledge students, such as when summarizing dense academic PDF papers; limited benefit for novice learners

## The 5-Phase System for Exam Preparation

### Phase 1: First encounter (Day 0)

Attend the lecture or read the material for the first time. Your goal is not to memorize legal rules for finals or other facts immediately - it's to understand the structure and context, use proven techniques to memorize things faster, and calculate exactly how much to study within a structured university revision timetable. Take notes actively, asking yourself "why" and "how" throughout.

Action: Immediately after, upload your notes to StudyCards AI and generate your flashcard deck for that material. The earlier you create cards after first exposure, the better the card quality.

### Phase 2: First review (Day 1–2)

This is the most critical review. The Ebbinghaus forgetting curve drops most steeply in the first 24 hours. A short active recall session within 24 hours of learning preserves most of what you encoded.

Action: Study your new flashcards in Anki. Be honest about difficulty ratings. This session doesn't need to be long - 20–30 minutes is enough to prevent the initial retention crash.

### Phase 3: Spaced review (Days 3–30)

Follow Anki's spaced repetition schedule. Review your daily queue every day without fail. Cards you find easy get pushed out further; hard cards come back sooner. Each review strengthens the memory trace.

Action: 20–45 minutes of daily reviews. Add new cards from each new lecture concurrently. Don't let the queue build up - consistency beats intensity.

### Phase 4: Practice questions (2 weeks before exam)

Shift focus to applying knowledge under exam conditions. Past papers, practice question banks, and timed tests reveal exactly where your flashcard knowledge isn't yet translating to exam performance.

Action: Continue Anki reviews daily. Add new flashcards for any gaps revealed by practice questions. Focus new card creation on application-style questions, not just definitions.

### Phase 5: Final week

No new material. Review your Anki queue (which will be light at this point - you've been spaced-reviewing everything). Focus on past papers and any weak areas identified.

Action: Prioritise sleep. Research consistently shows that sleep-deprived performance is significantly worse, and that reviewing before sleep is especially effective for consolidation.

## How Many Hours Should You Study for an Exam?

This depends entirely on the exam, not on a general rule. But here's what the research says about study time allocation:

- → Daily consistent study outperforms weekly marathons for retention. 1 hour per day for 30 days beats 30 hours in the final weekend.
- → Active recall time is more valuable than passive time. 2 hours of flashcard review typically outperforms 4 hours of re-reading in terms of exam outcomes.
- → Use our study hours calculator to estimate the time you need based on subject difficulty and exam date.

Use the Study Hours Calculator →

## The Power of Interleaving: Why Mixing Topics Beats Blocking

Most students use "blocked practice," where they study all of Topic A, then move to Topic B, and finally Topic C. While this creates a feeling of immediate mastery, it is often a mirage. Cognitive science suggests a superior alternative: Interleaving. This involves mixing different topics or types of problems within a single study session, forcing the brain to constantly reset and identify which strategy is required for a specific problem.

Interleaving is particularly effective for mathematics, physics, and language learning because it trains your brain to distinguish between different patterns. Instead of knowing *how* to use a formula (which is easy when you've done ten identical problems in a row), you learn *when* to use it—which is exactly what is required during a high-stakes exam.

- Avoid spending more than 60-90 minutes on a single sub-topic.
- Shuffle your practice question sets so that different chapters are mixed together.
- When using flashcards, ensure your deck is randomized rather than grouped by chapter.

## Breaking the Illusion of Competence

The biggest danger in exam preparation is the "Illusion of Competence." This occurs when you review material—such as re-reading a textbook or looking over highlighted notes—and mistake the ease of recognition for the ability to recall. Because the information looks familiar, your brain tricks you into believing you have mastered the content, leading to a "shock" during the exam when the cues from the textbook are gone.

To break this illusion, you must shift from passive recognition to active retrieval. The only way to truly verify knowledge is to attempt to retrieve it from memory without looking at the answer. This is why converting static notes into dynamic testing tools is essential. By using StudyCards AI to transform your lecture notes into active recall prompts, you force your brain to work, which signals to the mind that this information is important and needs to be permanently stored.

- The "Blank Page" Test: Try to write everything you know about a topic on a blank sheet before opening your notes.
- Teach it to a peer: If you cannot explain a concept simply, you have a gap in your understanding.
- Prioritize "desirable difficulty": If the study session feels easy, you probably aren't learning.

## Optimizing the Biological Hardware for Memory

No amount of science-backed study techniques can override the biological requirements of the brain. Memory is not just a cognitive process; it is a physical one. Long-term potentiation (the strengthening of synapses) happens primarily during sleep, not during the study session itself. When you study, you are essentially "marking" information for storage; when you sleep, your brain actually "writes" that information into your long-term memory.

Pulling an all-nighter is counterproductive because it eliminates the REM and deep sleep stages necessary for memory consolidation. Furthermore, the brain requires steady glucose levels and hydration to maintain the focus required for deep work. A dehydrated brain experiences a significant drop in cognitive processing speed and executive function.

- Maintain a consistent sleep schedule of 7-9 hours, especially during the "Spaced Review" phase.
- Use the 50/10 rule: 50 minutes of deep focus followed by 10 minutes of complete mental detachment (no phone).
- Hydrate consistently; even mild dehydration can impair short-term memory and concentration.

## Build Your Science-Backed Study System

StudyCards AI generates your flashcard deck from any notes in seconds. Export to Anki. Follow the system above. Stop re-reading - start retrieving.

Start Free → Spaced Repetition Schedule Guide

References

1. Dunlosky, J., Rawson, K.A., Marsh, E.J., Nathan, M.J., & Willingham, D.T. (2013). Improving students' learning with effective learning techniques. Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 14(1), 4–58.
